title: How to Add Mx Records in Hostinger Domain, Connect Gmail, Zoho Mail & Other 2026
description:
In today’s video, I’m going to teach you How to Add Mx Records in Hostinger Domain, Connect Gmail, Zoho Mail & Other 2026 — a crucial step for anyone looking to route emails properly through custom domains in 2026.

Whether you’re trying to connect your domain to Gmail, Zoho Mail, or any third-party provider, setting up custom MX records is essential to make sure your email works without a hitch.

Let’s dive in.

Step 1: Log in to Hostinger
Start by opening your browser and heading to hostinger.com. On the homepage, click Log In at the top right.

If you don’t have an account yet, you’ll need to sign up. But assuming you're already a user, just enter your credentials to get into the Hostinger dashboard, also known as the hPanel.

Step 2: Go to Domain Settings
Once inside the hPanel, click on the Domains section. This will open your Domain Portfolio, where you can see all the domains connected to your Hostinger account.

Locate the domain you want to manage and click on the Manage button.

Step 3: Access DNS Zone
Now you’re on the Domain Management page. Look for the DNS / Name Server settings or DNS Zone Editor. This is where you’ll handle all domain-level configurations, including MX record setup for Gmail or other providers.

Step 4: Add or Update MX Records
Inside the DNS panel, find the section where you can add new DNS entries. Click on the Type dropdown and select MX. This means you're working on Mail Exchange records, which determine where emails sent to your domain go.

You’ll now enter the following:

Mail server value (e.g., aspmx.l.google.com for Gmail or another provider’s server)

Priority (e.g., 1, 5, or 10)

TTL (use the default or set a custom time like 3600 seconds)

Click Add Record or Save Changes.

This step is key if you're setting up custom domain email routing or trying to point email from Hostinger to Google Workspace, Zoho Mail, or other platforms.

Step 5: Confirm Email Configuration
Once you’ve added the record, wait a few minutes to a few hours for DNS propagation to complete.

You can test if everything is working by sending a test email or using a DNS lookup tool to verify that your Hostinger MX entry setup is active.

Wrap-Up
And that’s it! You’ve just learned how to add MX records in Hostinger domain, helping you connect your domain to external email services.

This is one of the most important steps in managing professional email on your website, whether for personal branding or business.

Thanks for watching! If this helped you, don’t forget to like the video, leave a comment, and subscribe for more tutorials on Hostinger DNS setup, domain management, email configuration, and more.

title: How To Take Partial Profit in TradingView, Close Part Position Only 2026
description:
Here I will show you How To Take Partial Profit in TradingView, Close Part Position Only 2026 

In today's video, I'm going to guide you on how to take partial profit in TradingView quickly and easily. Whether you're new to trading or an experienced user on TradingView, managing your trades effectively by taking partial profits is a key component of a successful trading strategy. So, let's dive in. 

First, head over to tradingview.com and sign into your account. Once logged in, navigate to your chart. If you're setting up a new chart, select your desired symbol to begin market analysis. Now, let's use TradingView's paper trading feature, which is an excellent tool for practicing trades without any financial risk. Access it by clicking on paper trading at the bottom of the screen. This feature is perfect for simulating trades and testing strategies, especially if you're still honing your tactics before entering live markets.

In paper trading mode, click on positions to view your current trades. Here, you'll see all your active positions. Identify the position you'd like to partially close. This is beneficial if you want to lock in profits on a winning trade while keeping a portion open to benefit from any additional market upside. Click on the position you wish to partially close. A menu will appear with the option to close your position.

Before fully closing it, here's how to take partial profit: look for the partial close option, likely found under the main actions in the order options. Once located, toggle the partial close option. This allows you to decide how much of your position to close versus how much to keep open. Be strategic in determining the portion of your trade that aligns with your risk management and profit targets. Ensure the numerical values you choose fit within your trading plan and that you are comfortable with the amount left in play.

Finally, after setting the amount, click on partially close. This action secures some of your profits while allowing the remaining position to capture further market movements. Your adjustments should be reflected instantly, showcasing the effective capabilities of TradingView's platform. And there you have it, you've successfully taken a partial profit in TradingView.

Thank you for watching, and happy trading!"

title: How To Deploy GitHub Project on Vercel in 2026 Easily
description:
In this video, I have explained How To Deploy GitHub Project on Vercel in 2026 Easily step by step.

This is a complete Vercel deployment tutorial for beginners, or anyone who's curious about how to host a website or front-end project from GitHub using Vercel—for free.

🔹 Step 1: Create and Upload Your Project to GitHub
To begin, go to GitHub and create a new repository. In this tutorial, we’re building a simple to-do list web app, but the same method works for React apps, Next.js projects, or any static site deployment using Vercel.

I'll name the repository to-do. You can skip the description for now and click "Create Repository".

Now, we'll upload the project files. Go to the "Quick Setup" section, and instead of dragging and dropping, we’ll click "Upload files" and select files directly from your file manager. I'm uploading an index.html file for this basic HTML project. After selecting the files, click "Commit changes" to push them to GitHub.

🔹 Step 2: Connect GitHub to Vercel for Continuous Deployment
Now that your code is live on GitHub, go to Vercel.com—a popular platform for free hosting of GitHub projects, especially for front-end developers and JAMstack apps.

Click on "Start Deploying".

Next, click "Continue with GitHub". You’ll be asked to authenticate your GitHub account to link it with Vercel. If you're already logged in on another tab, Vercel will detect it automatically.

Click "Import Git Repository", then choose whether to grant Vercel access to all your GitHub repositories or just specific ones. For better control, I recommend selecting only specific repositories.

Select the to-do repository and click "Install".

🔹 Step 3: Configure and Deploy Your GitHub Project on Vercel
Once your repository is connected, Vercel will ask for some basic project settings:

Project Name: You can leave it as to-do

Framework Preset: Since this is a plain HTML project, choose "Other"

Now click "Deploy".

And just like that, Vercel will begin deploying your project. This is one of the easiest ways to host a GitHub project without backend, and it’s ideal for static site hosting, front-end prototyping, and free website deployment using Vercel.

🔹 Step 4: View Your Deployed Site on Vercel
Once the deployment is complete, you’ll get a live project URL.

Click "Visit" in the top-right corner, or simply use the domain generated by Vercel. Here’s the to-do list app we just uploaded—fully hosted and live.

For example, if I type “Record video” and hit Enter, it’s added to the list. It’s working perfectly.

🔹 Step 5: Managing Your Project on Vercel Dashboard
From the Vercel dashboard, you can manage all your deployed projects. Click the arrow next to your account name, select "Projects", and click into the one you just deployed.

You can view deployment logs, custom domain settings, environment variables, and more—all in one place.

Also, remember: any time you push updates to GitHub, Vercel automatically redeploys your project, making it perfect for continuous deployment workflows.

✅ Final Thoughts
And that’s it! You’ve now learned how to deploy a GitHub project on Vercel, even if you're a beginner.

This method works for:

Deploying static websites from GitHub

Hosting portfolio sites on Vercel

Deploying HTML, CSS, JavaScript projects

And even advanced use cases like Next.js deployment from GitHub to Vercel

If this helped you, please like, subscribe, and drop a comment if you have any questions.
